data.title: Value Line: Fairly Priced For Now
data.description:
Investment thesis

Shares of Value Line (NASDAQ:VALU) are down nearly 25% since my previous writeup where I stated that the stock was a Hold, citing its high valuation. While the share price decline has brought its valuation multiple down considerably, I argue that the current market environment where the major US indexes have seen significant declines so far this year, will put pressure on the company's asset management business going forward. The declining revenue and margins in its publishing business remains a major concern, which together with other risks I see, make VALU stock unappealing at the current valuation. My estimate for fair value is $39.40 per share, and I am therefore maintaining my Hold rating on the stock.

Decline in publishing revenue persists while asset management shines

As shown above, Q3 results for fiscal '25 showed that total publishing revenue was down 1.8% year over year. While the declines were slightly lower than those seen in the prior two quarters, income from publishing was down more than 30%. The lower margins were due to higher operating expenses, including higher advertising costs. This is indeed concerning, as the trend of lower revenue and margins is set to continue, as I see no clear path for this trend to reverse.

On the other hand, Value Line's asset management business EULAV, demonstrated impressive results, with overall income growing nearly 41% year over year, as shown above. This was likely driven by growth in assets under management, benefitting from the overall US stock market performing well towards the end of last year. Notably, asset management contribution flows directly to Value Line's bottom line after deductions for tax payments.

Business outlook and VALU stock valuation

Value Line's publishing business is set to bring in close to $7 million in net income for fiscal 2025. Given the declining revenues expected going forward, coupled with margins coming under pressure, I am inclined to adopt a conservative approach to valuing this business. A relevant peer like MarketWise (MKTW) which I covered recently with a Buy rating, has also struggled with generating organic growth and currently trades at only a high single digit FCF multiple. At the other end of the spectrum, a peer like Morningstar (MORN) which is demonstrating high single digit growth rates, is valued at over 30 times earnings. On the whole, I believe that a earnings multiple of 10 is justified for Value Line's publishing business, which puts its valuation at $70 million.

While some investors may argue that the publishing business deserves a lower valuation, I will point out that the key driver for VALU stock comes from its asset management segment. Net income from this segment is set to exceed $15 million for fiscal '25. However, these earnings can be quite volatile, and can fluctuate broadly in line with the stock market. As shown above, earnings fell sharply in 2023 due to weaker stock market performance in the prior year, which led to a decline in assets under management. Judging by this, the weaker stock market performance so far this year is likely to be a major headwind to earnings in the coming quarters.

Given the more volatile nature of these earnings, I believe the valuation multiple applied to it should represent a significant discount compared to private asset managers growing at similar rates such as StepStone Group (STEP) and Hamilton Lane (HLNE). These peers tend to trade at earnings multiples that are over 25. Meanwhile Artisan Partners Asset Management (APAM) which mainly invests in public equities and is growing at mid single digit rates, is valued at close to 11 times earnings. Taking this into consideration, if we assign a multiple of 15 to asset management earnings, this would put the valuation for this segment at $225 million. Notably, this is more than three times the value of the publishing business.

Now if we add the valuation of the two business units to the company's cash and short term investments of $76 million, we end up with a total value of $371 million. This translates to a share price of $39.40, based on 9.4 million shares outstanding. This implies that there is hardly any upside left from the current share price. I acknowledge that strong performance ahead in public market equities could significantly boost earnings and provide significant upside. On the contrary I also see meaningful risks that could negatively impact the current outlook, which I will walk through next.

Risks

Continued macroeconomic weakness could result in the overall stock market declining further, which would be a headwind to growth in assets under management. Furthermore, while the declining publishing revenue and margins is a concern, a bigger risk for investors is if the Value Line brand gradually loses its appeal due to this. Additionally, I would point that if we look at the performance of several of its funds such as the Value Line Select Growth Fund (VALSX) and Value Line Mid Cap Focused Fund (VLIFX), they have significantly underperformed the S&P 500 Index (SPX) over the past five years.

Other risks of note include the high revenue concentration in its publishing segment, with its largest customer accounting for 32.2% of total publishing revenues in fiscal 2024. It has also been disappointing to see capital returns to shareholders remaining muted, with the current dividend yield at just over 3%, with barely any share purchases done over the past few quarters. The lack of earnings calls and Wall Street coverage on the stock, make it challenging to understand management's vision and targets for the company.

Conclusion

While future investor returns hinge primarily on growth in its asset management business, the outlook for it does not look favorable given the ongoing market conditions. The current valuation for the stock remains unappealing considering the risks that I see. I am therefore sticking to the sidelines for now.

data.title: LexinFintech: My Thoughts On The Recent Sell-Off And Relative Value; Sell Recommendation
data.description:
Thesis

My recommendation for LexinFintech Holdings Ltd. (NASDAQ:LX) is a "sell". Amid the recent market downturn, the impact on LX has been even more pronounced. This has to do with several factors including the stock's reliance on market momentum in generating returns, high beta, and its small market capitalization of only $1.2 billion. If we assess the appeal of LX relative to its Chinese fintech peers, its valuation is difficult to justify. In today's environment with limited risk appetite, LX is perhaps not the best idea. If in case, you are a long-term investor with a high appetite for short-term volatility, I would be more inclined to invest in its competitors.

Company Background

LexinFintech is a fintech company based in China. It uses proprietary technology to help banks assess the credit risk of consumer borrowers, originate loans for banks, and service the loans. From 2020 to 2024, origination volumes per annum ranged from 177 billion CNY to 250 billion CNY (see the company's 4th quarter filings and CNY/USD = c7.3).

The company was founded in 2013 and went public in 2018. In 2018, total assets were around 12 billion CNY compared to 22 billion in 2024. Growth is pretty much in line with peers given the financial services industry's sensitivity to the overall macro environment and the similarity in the operating models of these fintech companies. Two close competitors are FinVolution Group (FINV) and Qifu Technology, Inc. (QFIN). Both have been exhibiting strong fundamentals, which we will dig into shortly.

Chart: LX exhibits strong 1 year momentum

Performance in the recent sell-off and technicals

As a small-cap (~$1bn) and high beta (c1.79) stock, LX had suffered a lot since the market turmoil in the aftermath of Trump's liberation day. Since the 8th of April, the S&P had recovered mildly from the slump as the VIX and term-premium implied recession probability came down. However, LX failed to follow. This could indicate a continued risk-off sentiment for stocks of its characteristics or a potential mis-pricing opportunity. If we look closer, the brief rebound in the market after Trump's 90-day tariff pause has highlighted returns in more aggressive factors such as momentum and growth. Both are quite relevant for LX, especially momentum with its 273% 1-year price performance. So, the decline for LX is likely not due to systematic factors.

You might ask, since this retaliation puts China in the spotlight, does the underperformance for LX or any of its peers for that matter have to do with China's risk? Mostly no, as we've seen a robust recovery for China ETFs such as FXI, CHIQ, and the overall Chinese equities index, CSI300. Therefore, the persistent and intense decline of LX and its peers is a bit of a conundrum.

The risk factors (e.g., momentum, small-cap) don't really support the underperformance of LX and its entire peers, it isn't completely about the showdown between the US and China, then what can it be about? Here are two factors that I'd like to discuss:

1. Market efficiency - An overall rebound is difficult to justify: As the geopolitical pressure mounts between the US and China, it is quite unexpected for me to see the bullish performance of Chinese equities during the same period. It could be due to the fact that previous sell-offs were driven by market risk premiums instead of fundamental expectations of Chinese companies.

The market went up on the 8th of April, followed by a minor downturn the next day. Meanwhile, long-term China treasury yields remain suppressed while the spike in long-term US treasury yields could be due to a technical sell-off instead of a reflection of the market expectation of growth. The persistent rise in corporate IG and HY bond yields also voiced concerns on fundamentals. Overall, there are still ample risks ahead. So, even if LX did not follow the brief market rebound, it doesn't mean that it should.

2. Technicals: What stands out for LX is its 1.79 beta value (24-month), 4.45% short interest, and a relatively high 3-month volume to its shares outstanding (~2.5%). All of these make the stock relatively vulnerable to a downturn and more volatile, especially in an environment of limited clarity. Nonetheless, the downward pressure of the stock due to the mentioned aspect will likely be temporary with a 60-month beta level of 0.36. But for now, those technical concerns are important risks to highlight.

Reflecting on those conditions, it doesn't make me much positive on the stock and to think that there will be short-term upsides. There is still much uncertainty in general and for the stock individually. These reasons alone already form a large part of my bearish thesis on the stock.

Fundamental and peer analysis

In the aftermath of the market turmoil since April, valuations for LX and its peers started to converge. However, LexinFintech is still relatively more expensive which, I think, is not justified. Here are my reasons:

1. Growth expectations: Forward p/e ratio for LX points to potential value. However, that is misguided due to excessive earnings expectations while revenue growth expectations are in line with peers.

2. Asset quality lags peers: In comparison with two fintech peers FinVolution Group (FINV) and Qifu Technology, Inc. (QFIN), LexinFintech has generally shown a higher 90+ days delinquency ratio. If the high earnings growth expectation is based on the compression of credit costs to an extent that is much stronger than competitors, it is unlikely.

3. Higher cost of leverage: Lastly, LexinFintech faces a much higher net debt in its capital structure, which further limits earnings growth. The limitation on earnings also has to do with its relatively lower profitability.

Relevant metrics summarizing the above points

Conclusion

My bearish thesis on LexinFintech is formed by three legs: 1) market risks are quite pronounced considering geopolitics and macro fundamentals moving forward 2) the technical features of the stock and its sensitivity to volatility make it an unfavourable bet 3) relative valuation is weak. On the other hand, I am overall bullish on Chinese banking and fintech considering China's easing credit cycle and the sector's offer on value. To that end, I'd recommend a look at its peers and my recent article on FinVolution.

data.title: Dow Jones, S&P 500 Forecast: Tech Tariff Pause Boosts Sentiment But Risks Remain
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Technology stocks surged, boosting Asian and European shares on Monday, after President Trump excluded smartphones and computers from China tariffs. This gave markets a breather after a week of sharp ups and downs.

Wall Street Indexes look set to follow suit, with gains expected for major tech giants. There was still uncertainty as President Trump said on Sunday that tariffs on semiconductors would be announced within a week, and a decision on phones would come "soon."

Having finished last week on a positive note, market participants will be hoping for further gains this week. There are however, a host of risks to consider and markets are by no means out of the woods just yet. Expect further volatility as the tariff picture continues to develop.

On Friday last week, the fear and greed index was around the 13 mark which leaves it in extreme fear territory despite the positive end to the week. This should not come as a surprise as the recent US equity market sell-off ranks among the most severe short-term declines since 1929.

Monday will be a quiet day in terms of data, but tariff announcements or updates may move the needle for US indices. There is also a host of Federal Reserve policymakers on the docket later in the day which could add further uncertainty for market participants to navigate.

Technical Analysis - Dow Jones and S&P 500

Dow Jones (US30) Daily Chart, April 14, 2025

From a technical standpoint, the Dow Jones finished last week with a gain of around 5.22% and gapped higher over the weekend.

The gap higher down to the developments over the weekend regarding technology equipment being exempt from tariffs, for the time being.

The 14-period RSI on a daily timeframe may be a good indicator to keep an eye on. The RSI is currently approaching the 50 neutral level with a rejection likely to lead to fresh lows and a sign that momentum remains in favor of bears. A break above the 50 mark may be seen as a sign of a shift in momentum and could embolden bulls.

Should bulls prevail, markets will be eyeing 41095 and 41400 before the 41950 handle comes into focus.

A bearish push from here may find support at the psychological 40000 mark before 39588 and 39000 come into focus.

Support

  • 40000
  • 39588
  • 39000

Resistance

  • 41095
  • 41400
  • 41950

S&P 500

Looking at the S&P 500 and the chart almost mirrors the Dow Jones.

I will also be paying close attention to the period-14 RSI on a daily timeframe to monitor any potential momentum shifts.

A break higher does face some crucial levels with the 5500 handle also hosting the 20-day MA before markets eye 5538 and 5635.

A push lower for the S&P 500 and markets will look for potential support at 5391, 5330 and 5267 respectively.

For both indexes tariff developments and overall sentiment hold the keys. Any moves will largely depend on how this develops over the course of the day and week ahead.

S&P 500 Daily Chart, April 14, 2025

Support

  • 5391
  • 5330
  • 5267

Resistance

  • 5500
  • 5538
  • 5635

Original Post

data.title: Robinhood: Cash-Rich And Ready To Run
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Robinhood Markets, Inc. (NASDAQ:HOOD) has a lot going for it. For one, the business is highly innovative, consistently launching new products to satisfy its rapidly growing customer base.

But what makes Robinhood particularly compelling is that it's highly successful at monetizing its user base to grow its revenue line.

What's more, the business generates a lot of free cash flow, which I estimate could reach around $1.5 billion of free cash flow in 2025.

For this debt-free business, paying around 26x forward free cash flow strikes me as a bargain.

Rapid Recap

Robinhood is a stock that I'd been bullish on and recommended to Deep Value Returns subscribers. Here's the date I recommended the stock and the date I sold the stock.

At the time that I recommended selling the stock HOOD was approximately $39 per share, nailing down a large gain in less than 1 year. And what now?

Robinhood's Near-Term Prospects

Robinhood makes it super easy for traders to trade stocks, options, and crypto.

What makes Robinhood stand out is how simple and user-friendly it is. There are no trading commissions, and they’ve packed in a bunch of tools that help trade a lot and quickly.

Furthermore, they’ve recently added a premium credit card as well as better tools for serious traders.

Basically, Robinhood wants to be the go-to financial app for the next generation, offering everything from investing and borrowing to crypto, as a super-app.

Keep in mind that Robinhood's Q4 2024 just saw their first-ever $1 billion revenue quarter, and they’re planning more growth by rolling out new features, including expanding their crypto offerings.

Altogether, they’ve got three significant product launches this year—one focused on their Gold membership, another on crypto, and a third for active traders.

All sounds great, right? Well, here's one part of the problem.

For the month of February, we saw Assets Under Custody shrink by 8% y/y. This means that as the market goes down, as you may expect, Assets Under Custody also go down.

And if we were to presume that markets remain volatile for a while, this could impact its ability to grow its assets under custody, which would impact its ability to grow its revenues. Given this context, let's now discuss its financials.

Revenue Growth Rates Likely to Moderate

Robinhood isn't able to provide much in the way of tangible guidance, given how volatile markets can be.

What they do provide is some vague outlook that the business should grow its revenues by at least double-digit.

However, I suspect that most investors have already expected and priced in this sort of growth rate in 2025, long ago.

Consequently, now the crucial question is this. Do double-digit growth rates translate into 10% y/y revenue growth rates? Or 15% y/y? Or potentially even as much as 25% y/y revenue growth rates?

Of course, it goes without saying, the stronger the growth rates the business delivers, the higher the multiple its stock will get.

The one blemish to think about here is that Q4 2024 saw particularly strong revenues, which will make this year's Q4 2025 up against a very challenging period.

But again, the main question is this, for the year as a whole, can investors get conviction that Robinhood could potentially grow at around 15% CAGR? Because if Robinhood manages to deliver around 15% topline growth today, the stock is already attractively priced.

HOOD Stock Valuation -- 26x Forward Free Cash Flow

As an Inflection investor, I'm drawn to the fact that Robinhood's balance sheet holds no debt. Case in point, even though there are approximately $3.5 billion of securities loaned, these are roughly balanced against its securities segregated on its balance sheet, under current assets.

Simply put, Robinhood holds about $5 billion of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet, with no debt. This means that more than 12% of its market cap is made up of cash, with no debt against that cash. This leaves Robinhood with the flexibility to either reinvest in its operations as it shows an inclination to do, or to repurchase its shares.

Moving on, Robinhood delivered about $1.2 billion of free cash flow in 2025. Thus, if we assume that together with approximately a 15% topline increase in its revenues this year, Robinhood's free cash flow increases by approximately 20% y/y, this would see Robinhood delivering about $1.5 billion of free cash flow in 2025.

This means that the stock is priced at 26x forward free cash flow. A figure that I believe to be very attractive, given its expected 15% topline growth this year, together with the fact that it holds no debt on its balance sheet.

Risk Factors to Consider

As alluded to earlier, the main question investors have to contend with here is what could be the fallout of interest rates on traders' volume.

Here's a quote from the earnings call that highlights this risk factor:

We are seeing that relationship, that natural hedge play out where rates go one direction and trading goes the other.

Another risk factor to keep in mind is that Robinhood's expansion into credit cards and lending which means more regulation and potential credit losses if customers don’t repay.

Their growth in lending, especially, has to be balanced carefully, with companies like SoFi Technologies, Inc. (SOFI) chasing similar users, and vying for market share too.

The Bottom Line

At the end of the day, I'm bullish on Robinhood because the financials just make sense.

The company is debt-free, sitting on about $5 billion in cash and securities, and it’s generating serious free cash flow—potentially $1.5 billion in 2025.

When I look at that and see it trading at just 26x forward free cash flow, for a company still growing its top line by double digits, it feels like a compelling setup.

There’s real value here if you’re willing to look past the short-term volatility.

data.title: Renesas Likely Meaningfully Undervalued, But Tariffs And Trade Chaos Create Risks
data.description:
The best I can say about the performance of leading Japanese semiconductor company Renesas Electronics (OTCPK:RNECY) (OTCPK:RNECF) (6723.T) is that it definitely could have been worse. Down about 15% since my last article (and 10% for the U.S. ADRs), Renesas did at least do better than the broader semi sector and specific competitors like Analog Devices (ADI), Infineon Technologies (OTCQX:IFNNY), Microchip Technology (MCHP), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), STMicroelectronics (STM), and Texas Instruments (TXN).

With market share gains, new products ramping, and an improved outlook for auto sales in Asian markets, as well as some improvement in industrial markets, there are some positives in Renesas’s outlook. On the other hand, U.S. tariffs have created exceptional chaos and may not only directly impact the price competitiveness of Renesas’s products (and their customers’ products), but also drive lower overall economic activity due to significantly greater uncertainty in the market.

If long-term revenue growth of 5% is still a valid expectation (and I believe it is), and Renesas can return its free cash flow margins to the low/mid-20%’s, the shares are likely 50% or more undervalued today, making this a name worth consideration for investors who can accept the above-average risk that is present today.

2025 Is Starting To Look More “Less Bad” Than “Better”

It’s no secret that the semiconductor industry had a tough 2024 (at least outside of direct data center/AI products), as end-user demand in major segments like autos and industrial weakened through the year. At the same time, both end-users and distributors worked down their inventories in response to not only soft immediate demand, but increased caution and uncertainty regarding a near-term recovery.

Overall microcontroller sales declined 22% in 2024, with an 11% decline in auto MCUs and a 47% decline MCUs for industrial applications. While not all of Renesas’s revenue comes from MCUs (it’s around 40% to 50% now), it’s still a significant part of the business, and it’s not as if analog or power semiconductor markets were remarkably better.

At this point, that expected and hoped-for recovery in 2025 continues sliding to the right. The trade and tariff policies of the U.S. administration have created significant uncertainty and turmoil in global markets, and while European economies were already looking at sluggish trends for 2025, the outlook for the U.S. is slipping as well.

I’m very interested to see what Renesas reports with respect to earnings, guidance, and management commentary when it reports first quarter 2025 results on April 24. I won’t be surprised if the company benefits from accelerated orders meant to get ahead of tariffs and posts stronger revenue for the quarter, but I likewise won’t be surprised if management is considerably more cautious with its guidance for the remainder of 2025.

Sales to auto customers are around half of Renesas’s revenue base, and while the outlook for passenger vehicle markets in Japan, China, and the rest of Asia are actually pretty healthy right now, the outlook for Europe and North America is weakening, with the imposition of tariffs likely to make affordability for U.S. customers a much more significant issue.

At the same time, quantifying the company’s total exposure to U.S. tariffs is challenging – while only about 10% to 15% of the company’s revenue comes from the U.S., I assume that a meaningful percentage of its sales to customers in Japan, China, and the rest of Asia are for products ultimately exported to the U.S. (Japanese carmakers, for instance, generate about one-third of their revenue from the U.S., with production spread across Japan, the U.S., Canada, and Mexico).

The industrial markets that Renesas serves are no easier to predict at this point. While Renesas has good leverage to factory automation, the ever-changing tariff outlook is likely to lead many customers to further postpone capex investments. Likewise, demand for chips used in products like appliances could weaken after an initial surge intended to get ahead of those tariffs, and I expect IoT product demand could be meaningfully impacted by tariffs as well given that not many of those are assembled in the U.S.

While it is certainly true that the U.S. market is only part of Renesas’s overall market opportunity, overall industrial capex is likely to remain depressed and companies are likely to remain cautious until there is more clarity on what the new “rules” are going to be.

Post-Chaos, There Are Still Growth Drivers

It may be more bullish than I realize to assume that there will be a new steady-state at some point (as opposed to years of ongoing chaos and unpredictability), but even so, I do think there are some solid internal positives for Renesas that position it favorably relative to many of its peers.

Renesas has continued to advance its system-on-a-chip capabilities for auto customers, with its 3nm R-Car SoC not only facilitating centralized electronic control units, but allowing automakers to take a “modular” approach to car design that allows multiple different products to be made with the same core components – in other words, the design of Renesas’s new chips allows OEMs to build economy, mid-range, and high-end vehicles from the same basic “backbone”, adding/upgrading components as desired for various price and performance ranges.

The company also continues to position itself as a leader in the emerging software-defined vehicle space. The company recently announced a multiyear development deal with Honda Motor Co. (HMC) to develop new high-performance SoCs for software-defined vehicles, and I expect the company’s leading-edge offerings and deep experience in auto SoCs to serve it well as the industry transitions to this approach.

I also continue to see opportunities outside the auto market. Renesas has been gaining some share in power semiconductors for data center servers, and I likewise see further growth opportunities in DDR5-related products like memory interfaces and power management chips. Longer term, automation and IoT remain meaningful opportunities for the company, particularly as it can offer high-end, high-performance MCUs and SoCs as well as complementary products like PMICs and memory interfaces.

The company is also doing alright from a share perspective. As per the most recent Gartner report, Renesas gained share in the MCU market in 2024. Although Infineon and NXP did better (gaining more than three points, and almost three points, respectively), Renesas nevertheless gained almost a point of share in 2024, bringing it up to almost 16% (versus 20% for Infineon, 19% for NXPI, 13% for STMicro, and 11% for Microchip).

Renesas still punches above its weight in auto MCUs (24% share versus 32% at Infineon and 21% at NXPI), but I believe at least some of the relative weakness in its industrial MCU business in 2024 (about 8% share, down almost two points) was due to particular customer and end-market exposures.

The Outlook

Weak demand from end-users and distributors has led to soft capacity utilization numbers at Renesas (around 35% in Q4 2024 and expected to be in the low-40%s in Q1 2025), but the company has nevertheless managed to maintain respectable margins. Non-GAAP gross margin of 56% in FY 2024 compares pretty well to peers (ranging from 39% at STM and 42% at Infineon to 58% at NXPI and Texas Instruments), and likewise with 29% non-GAAP operating margin (versus 35% at NXPI, 34% at TXN, 17% at Infineon, and 11% at STMicro).

Given my expectations for weak revenue in FY 2025, I don’t expect meaningful utilization or margin improvement, but if and when revenue recovers in FY 2026 and beyond, there should be meaningful margin leverage in the business and a return to low-to-mid-30%’s OPM in FY 2026 or FY 2027.

I’m expecting a slight (around 1%-1.5%) decline in revenue in FY 2025, and I’m about 3% below the Street at this point. I’m likewise below the Street in FY 2026 (rev up 11%), and I’m expecting long-term annualized growth around 5% against a trailing average closer to 6% (though that number includes M&A).

Although I’m modeling about a half-point of OPM erosion in FY 2025, I do think the company could maintain flat margins with a little more focus on operating efficiency (with that ever-present tension between maximizing near-term profitability while not compromising future growth). I’m expecting a return to 30%+ OPM in FY 2026, and I believe that the company can return to 20%-plus free cash flow margin in FY 2025 and grow FCF at a double-digit annualized rate from an admittedly low 2024 starting point.

Discounted cash flow suggests more than 50% undervaluation today, and I get a similar result with a near-term margin and multiples-based approach. If Renesas can achieve a 29% non-GAAP OPM in 2025, a 4.25x forward revenue multiple would be fair (if not a little conservative) based upon what the market has traditionally paid for similar profitability. That too supports 50%-plus upside, but it is worth mentioning that the market is often very sensitive to short-term outlooks, so I’m not surprised the current uncertainties over trade policy and global economic growth have the shares trading well below what would otherwise seem like a fair multiple.

The Bottom Line

I’m not going to argue that Renesas is the best-run semiconductor company out there (Analog Devices, NXP, and Texas Instruments all have very strong cases), but I do think it is a well-run company with good exposure to growth drivers like industrial automation and electrification, as well as vehicle digitalization and electrification. I do see elevated near-term risk as businesses around the world try to adjust to the new realities of U.S. policy, but Renesas is a diverse, well-run company and I think today’s valuation sets the stage for good long-term returns for investors willing to take on that elevated risk today.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Graco: A Premium Player Is Seeing A Soft Patch
data.description:
Last summer, I concluded that Graco Inc. (NYSE:GGG) was a premium business which deserved a premium valuation. The fluid and coating management system and equipment provider has been a solid value creator for investors in the long run.

What followed was a soft second half of 2024, with full year sales down, although the company has announced some interesting bolt-on deals in the meantime. Net, net, I am underwhelmed by the performance, which makes me cautious here as shares continue to command a premium valuation here.

Managing Fluids & Coatings

Graco provides products, equipment, and devices which allow for the management of fluids and coatings, these being used in industrial and commercial applications. These products allow for measurement, movement, control, and dispensing of fluid and powdered materials.

The company generates over $2 billion in revenues from the sale of these products, with nearly half of sales generated from contractor markets, complemented by industrial and process segments. Nearly two-thirds of sales are generated in the Americas, complemented by the EMEA region and Asia-Pacific.

End markets are mostly tied to construction, responsible for about half of sales, industrial & machine, responsible for about a sixth of sales, complemented by smaller exposure to automotive, vehicle, public works, oil & natural gas, among others.

One very fun statistic is that the business generates half of its sales from products, of which it sells just zero to one product per day, indicating how much the company focuses on specific products. This has paid off handsomely, as the company reports a 6% organic growth rate on average over the past two decades, while the company delivers superior capital and shareholder returns as well.

Given today's discussions, it is good to know that about 80% of products are produced in the US, as the company delivers great service to its clients amidst great in-stock metrics.

About The Valuation

Early in 2024, the company reported a 2.5% increase in 2023 sales to $2.20 billion, as the business is very profitable, with operating profits reported at $643 million, resulting in very impressive operating margins near 30% of sales. GAAP earnings of $507 million came in just shy of $3 per share.

The company started 2024 on a softer note with first quarter sales down 7%, accompanied by earnings declines, although the company continued to operate with a very conservative balance sheet, revealing a net cash position of nearly $600 million.

With shares trading at 25 times earnings over the summer, the company traded at a premium valuation, justified by the long-term outperformance, yet a decline at the start of 2024 did not inspire much confidence.

Trading Flat

Since last summer, shares of Graco have been trading in a relatively tight $70-$90 range, now trading at the middle of the range. After the softer first quarter, second quarter sales fell by just a percent.

In September, Graco announced a EUR 230 million deal to acquire Corob S.p.A, as earnouts have the potential to increase this transaction price by another EUR 30 million. Corob produces high-performance volumetric and gravimetric dispensing, mixing, and shaking equipment and adds about EUR 110 million in sales. Valued at just over 2 times sales, the multiple looks quite reasonable, although no margin details were announced.

The momentum provided by this deal could be welcomed as third quarter sales fell 4% again, but that did not withhold the company from increasing the dividend by nearly 8% to $0.275 per share, paid each quarter, for an annual payout of $1.10 per share.

In January, the company posted a 3% fall in fourth quarter sales, despite a 3% contribution from M&A, with full year sales down 4% to $2.11 billion. Net earnings fell by a similar 4% to $486 million, as GAAP earnings fell a similar 4% to $2.82 per share as well. Note that the GAAP earnings paint a prettier picture, than really is the case, as adjusted earnings were down 9% to $2.77 per share.

What About 2025?

After a rather disappointing 2024, the company guided for 2025 sales to increase by low single digits on an organic and constant currency basis, with both M&A and currencies likely adding on top of that growth. Unfortunately, the comments for growth is all the guidance which has been offered here.

Despite the M&A efforts, net cash is equal to $647 million, with these holdings only having grown throughout the year, pushing up the net cash position to nearly $4 per share. The issue is that if we exclude this from an $80 share price, the company trades at 27 times the adjusted earnings reported for 2024, after they were down nearly 10% last year, although those earnings are likely seen up this year.

Doing the modeling I can easily see revenues up to $2.2-$2.3 billion, in the absence of too much of an impact of the tariffs, either directly or indirectly, as earnings should likely improve towards $3 per share, but even if earnings could improve to that number, which is a big if, shares trade at 25 times earnings.

What Now?

I am surprised by the resilient performance of Graco Inc. shares, as the business performed soft on an organic basis in 2024.

This is offset by interesting M&A activity, yet overall, the modest gains shown by the shares surprise me in light of the soft organic growth performance, all of which makes me very cautious here.

Frankly, the pullback of the market makes the risk-reward in other segments of the market look more compelling than Graco Inc. stock here.

data.title: Royal Caribbean: Over-Feared Slowdown
data.description:
Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (NYSE:RCL) was riding the cruising wave higher before the sudden disruption in travel. The stock is already some $90 off the all-time highs due to fears of a slowdown in travel demand. My investment thesis is ultra-bullish on RCL stock due to the big discount on future growth plans, though a recession would definitely disrupt short-term results.

Travel Slowdown Fears

The big unknown currently impacting the cruise line stocks is how much vacation travel will slow down, if at all. The market is already seeing some signs of weaker business air travel due to the tariff wars, but this type of travel isn't the same as a cruise vacation.

Apollo Asset Management reported weaker daily air travel across the top 10 U.S. airports. However, TSA checkpoint numbers were around 2.7+ million travelers on the key weekend days of Thursday, Friday, and Sunday of the last weekend suggesting a travel slowdown doesn't exist with the numbers matching or topping last April.

The cruise sector hasn't seen the same weak signs yet and a lot of cruises are planned far in advance. Only a few weeks ago, Carnival Corporation & plc (CCL) reported strong cruise demand. The cruise line had 80% of rooms booked for 2025, with record booking levels for trips in 2026.

CCL, the largest cruise line by revenue, reported FQ1 earnings on March 21, so over 3 weeks have passed since this quarterly update on demand. Royal Caribbean will report key Q1 earnings on April 29, with the company starting off the quarter reporting 2025 bookings at load factors in line with prior years at higher rates.

The cruise line even announced plans to launch a premium river cruise vacation concept in 2027. Royal Caribbean committed to ordering 10 ships for the Celebrity River Cruises business in signs of solid growth opportunities ahead.

Perfecta Program

Back shortly after the end of the Covid shutdowns, management introduced the Trifecta program in late 2022. The stock traded around only $55 despite the company targeting a $10 EPS by 2025.

Eventually, the stock soared and recently reached nearly $280. The market probably overreacted to the excitement around the bullish cruise business outlook without a balanced view of normal economic disruptions.

Regardless, Royal Caribbean came out with new financial targets after hitting the previous targets early as follows:

  • 20% compound annual growth rate in Adjusted Earnings per Share compared to 2024.
  • Return on Invested Capital ("ROIC") in the high teens.

The Perfecta program starts with a $11.80 EPS base in 2024. Royal Caribbean would have to hit the following EPS targets to reach 20% annual EPS growth.

  • 2025 - $14.75.
  • 2026 - $18.44.
  • 2027 - $23.05.

The current trade wars could very well disrupt these financial plans with a recession denting vacation demand. Management had targeted ~23% EPS growth this year with a $14.50 EPS target.

The stock has suddenly fallen to only $190 in a sign of how a disconnect exists between the long-term potential of the cruise line and a potential short-term hiccup in vacation demand. Royal Caribbean only trades at 13x consensus EPS targets up at nearly $15 with the corporate target just slightly below analysts.

The consensus analyst estimates only forecast a 2027 EPS target of $20 versus the equivalent of a $23 target to hit the internal Perfecta goals of management for 20% EPS growth. Either way, Royal Caribbean is not priced to reach the much lower consensus targets making the stock an extreme bargain.

The cruise line is in such a better position now that Royal Caribbean recently announced spending $100 million on share buybacks. The company even has a $3 annual dividend with a 1.6% dividend yield due to the far better balance sheet only a few years after restarting operations from the Covid shutdowns.

Royal Caribbean has net debt of nearly $20 billion, but the net PP&E sits up at nearly $32 billion now. The cruise line generated $6 billion in adjusted EBITDA last year.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Royal Caribbean is already pricing in a major slowdown in cruise demand, which may not actually occur. The stock trades at just 13x EPS targets with growth rates targeted at 20% suggesting any downturn in demand is already priced into the stock, though Royal Caribbean can always trade lower in the short term on a feared slowdown.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Delisting Deja Vu: Tariffs Are Putting Alibaba's Valuation At Risk Again
data.description:
Investor angst in Chinese equities is high once again, with the cohort’s early-year rally now proving to be another false dawn. Just when markets thought things couldn't get worse than the Liberation Day reciprocal tariff announcements, the U.S.-China standoff proved otherwise. What started off as a 54% reciprocal tariff on China imports has now become 145% after Beijing's ongoing enforcement of retaliatory measures, which now include a 125% counter-levy on U.S. goods. Beijing’s gone as far as to vow it’ll “fight to the end” on what it calls unilateral aggression from the U.S. The remarks represent a clear inflection point of how Beijing wants to deal with the aggressive U.S. trade war – there’s no backing down – which remains underappreciated.

As the U.S.-China trade war escalates, Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA) finds itself prone to imminent downside risks beyond its control once again. Despite company-specific strengths found in AI and a business model that’s bound to benefit from Beijing’s recent policy support for innovation and boosting domestic consumption, Alibaba remains exposed in the U.S.-China crossfire. This is evident in the stock’s 30%+ plummet over the past two weeks – what started as potentially a brief stint of profit-taking following the early-year AI rally has taken a steep downturn since the tariff spat began in early April.

While some see opportunity in the wreckage, it’s undoubtedly that Alibaba’s fundamental outlook and underlying risk premium remains exposed to ongoing uncertainties in the U.S.-China tariff war. Despite recent signs of fundamental improvement stemming from Alibaba’s internal reorganization, and favourable policy support from Beijing for its core commerce and AI businesses, the unrelenting tariff pressures remain a significant challenge that can outweigh its strengths.

The unrelenting tariff pressures are likely to become a protracted and significant challenge. This uncertainty represents significant and imminent multiple compression risks that shouldn’t be overshadowed by recent signs of fundamental improvement stemming from Beijing’s favourable policy support for its core commerce and AI business. As a result, we believe the stock could find itself back in the $80-level support range, which would represent a further downside of as much as 20% until the intensifying U.S.-China trade tensions subside.

Don’t Underestimate Trade War Uncertainties

The latest salvo in the U.S.-China trade war has now become a full-blown crisis that sheds significant uncertainties to Alibaba’s outlook due to its international trade exposure. China’s “fight to the end” mentality has not only triggered the U.S. to up the reciprocal tariffs to an unprecedented 145%, but it’s also brought back delisting risks onto the table – something that, we thought, was resolved once and for all after Beijing opted to comply with the Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act in 2022.

In response to the 145% reciprocal tariffs that went into effect on April 10, Beijing further increased its taxes on U.S. goods from 34% to 84% then 125% as of April 11. And instead of hitting back with another hike to the reciprocal tariffs, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent’s now announced the prospects of “removing Chinese companies from American stock exchanges”.

The impact of tariffs on Alibaba’s international business is one thing, but uncertainties that come with delisting risks are another.

Assessing the Tariff’s Fundamental Impact

Although Alibaba does not separately disclose its revenues by geography, the company generates at least 12% of its revenues overseas based on total sales reported for its Alibaba International Digital Commerce (“AIDC”) segment alone. Its largest exposure stems from AliExpress – Alibaba’s international e-commerce arm, which management’s been doubling down on in the U.S. in particular with bargain initiatives like AE Choice to fend competition like PDD Holdings Inc.’s (PDD) Temu and Shein.

The latest tariffs and President Trump’s removal of the “de minimis” rule are bound to impact the overseas unit’s growth outlook, which has been a key driver of the AIDC segment’s recent outperformance. Specifically, U.S. consumers have been resonating with the China-buying sensation in recent years, thanks to “cheap thrills” offered across all goods categories, with fast and free shipping perks. The combination of competitive pricing and shipping perks has incentivized the popularity of Chinese-operated e-commerce platforms, spanning AliExpress, Temu and Shein, which have outshone the merely “Made in China” goods offered on others like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN).

But that’s about to change. Although AliExpress’s U.S. exposure remains in a distant third place compared to its comparable Chinese rivals like PDD’s Temu and Shein, the upcoming changes to the “de minimis” exemption and import tariffs are bound to upend the unit’s growth outlook and earnings profile.

Specifically, the de minimis rule had historically exempted shipments valued at under $800 into the U.S. from tariffs. This relief, which has been critical for overseas e-commerce platforms like AliExpress, is set to be removed beginning May 2. Any shipment that falls below the $800 threshold into the U.S. will now also be subjected to a series of escalated charges. Initially, the new de minimis duty was set at either a 30% tariff or a per-item charge of $25 instead, which will be increased to $50 after June 1. But due to the escalating tariff fallout with China, President Trump's increased the de minimis levy to 120%, or a per-item charge of $100 starting May 1, which will be increased to $200 after June 1. This is expected to upend the unit economics of buying from China from American consumers, and significantly deter purchases moving forward. Even with efforts in stockpiling inventory in U.S. warehouses to bypass the de minimis exemption disruptions, the cost basis of related goods will eventually be upended by the 145% reciprocal tariffs, which, if passed onto consumers, will deter purchases, nonetheless.

As a result, Alibaba’s growth outlook will now depend further on domestic consumption at a time when local economic prospects remain mixed. Admittedly, Alibaba is the most comprehensive e-commerce platform in China. The Taobao platform is known for its wide variety of goods at diverse price points, while Tmall penetrates demand for premium goods that depend on brand confidence. This has made Alibaba’s platforms the preferred destination for local consumers as they look to cash in on the generous trade-in subsidies, which target big-ticket items like consumer electronics and home appliances.

But this is only a drop in the bucket when compared to the broader implications of tariff uncertainties. In addition to the direct impact on AliExpress’ growth and earnings outlook, Alibaba is poised to reel from the indirect impact on the broader Chinese economy as well. Almost a fifth of China’s GDP is dependent on exports. And in 2024, China’s accounted for almost 19% of total U.S. imports, valued at more than $500 billion. With the China economy still reeling from pandemic-era woes and an ongoing property slump, the last thing it needs are the 104% tariffs imposed by the country to which it exports the most to.

Although the trade war might incentivize the central government to further encourage domestic consumption, the stakes are high for consumers. Despite Chinese household savings having hit a record high last year, local consumers remained skeptical about discretionary spending in the face of ongoing property deterioration. The asset class has been a structural component to Chinese wealth creation, hence the destructive impact that the property slump’s had on the local economy in recent years. The impact was also evident in the protracted streak of quarterly deceleration in Alibaba’s core commerce revenues in recent years. And now given the added impact of the U.S.-China tariff fallout, consumers are poised to curtail discretionary spending once again, which threatens to overturn Alibaba’s core commerce recovery and adversely impact its valuation prospects.

Navigating the Unquantifiable from Tariffs

The silver lining is that the fundamental implications of tariffs will eventually be quantifiable. What’s scarier are factors that aren’t quantifiable – and this exists as a byproduct of the ongoing trade war. In response to China’s latest increase in levies on U.S. imports to 84%, the U.S. has brought delisting risks on Chinese stocks from American exchanges back on the table.

The last time this happened was when the Trump administration announced the HFCAA, which, at the time, aimed at targeting Beijing’s refusal to open up the books of U.S.-listed China firms for PCAOB inspection. The double-whammy of delisting risks threatened by the U.S. government, and macroeconomic deterioration and regulatory uncertainties in China had caused Alibaba to plummet 80% in value from its peak before HFCAA compliance was received and any signs of recovery emerged.

A similar setup is now in play. In addition to ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties in China, the added overhang of U.S. delisting risks could throw Alibaba’s valuation back into previous lows. Specifically, the delisting risks are bound to impact Alibaba’s valuation outlook not through its fundamentals, but rather through the risk premium priced into its multiple. To tangibly quantify the impact is almost impossible, as delisting risks harbinger a multitude of implications, with the most adverse being reduced institutional appetite for the stock.

At the peak of delisting fears, and China-related macroeconomic and regulatory woes facing Chinese tech shares in 2022, the cohort – including Alibaba – was deemed “uninvestable” by major funds. This was largely the culprit of ballooning risk premiums for Chinese equities, which drove widening valuation discounts to their U.S. counterparts at the time. Although Alibaba’s fundamentals may have been showing signs of improvement in the face of local policy support for its core commerce and AI business, the risk of this adverse narrative repeating itself shouldn’t be entirely dismissed – especially given the ongoing U.S.-China tariff standoff’s now introducing implications that span beyond quantifiable impacts to fundamentals.

Conclusion

In the worst-case scenario, which is now a reasonable outcome amidst ongoing tariff uncertainties, we believe Alibaba’s valuation could revert to the previous support level found in the $80 range. As discussed in our previous coverages on the stock, the $80 range represents a level at which Alibaba’s downside fundamental prospects can still tangibly support. With previously considered upside catalysts – such as local support for consumption and innovation critical for Alibaba’s core commerce and AI businesses – now taking a back seat, markets may be on the verge of pricing the stock back to its “safety net” levels once again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: RTX Corporation: A Resilient Aerospace Stock To Buy
data.description:
Recently, I have started adjusting price targets for many stocks. In the semiconductor industry, we see some pressure on earnings estimates. For aerospace stocks, there is also some pressure but less prominent.

For General Electric Company (GE), I recently lowered my price target reflecting a lower market trading multiple. In this report, I will discuss why I believe RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX) stock remains a buy.

RTX Stock Has Shown Resilience

Since my last report in February, RTX stock has shown resilience gained 2.8% while the market lost 12.6% of its value. The stock price did dip to $117.30, but has bounced around 9% since then. Resilience is definitely one of the things I am looking for in a stock, and RTX seems to have that. That does not mean it is completely isolated from the current market turmoil driven by trade and macroeconomics, but it does make it a somewhat more preferable stock for investors that are looking for stable long-term opportunities.

Global And U.S. Defense Budgets Are Set To Grow

In the U.S., defense stocks have lost momentum on remarks that the current White House administration has been seeking to cut defense budgets. While that is somewhat worrisome, in my view the current global threat environment requires higher defense budgets and not lower budgets. Additionally, I do believe that some cost-cutting can happen at the government in the procurement process with possible cost savings by simplifying the bureaucratic process of procurement and establishing and validating lists of requirements.

We’re now also seeing the first signs of a push for higher defense spending and the U.S. is part of that. The U.S. is now seeking defense spending to be 5% of GDP. It will not happen overnight, but it does show that lower defense spending over the longer term is not the realistic path for NATO members. In Europe, defense spending is already ramping up. This is in part to reduce reliance on the U.S. However, for the time being, for many weapon systems, Europe will still rely on U.S. defense contractors, including RTX.

The recent selection of The Boeing Company (BA) to provide the next-generation stealth air superiority fighter, which will cost around $200 million per unit, is another sign that the U.S. requires higher defense spending. It also seems that President Trump is losing patience with Russia in relation to the peace talks, which are not showing any signs of significant progress and that could result in President Trump doubling down on defense spending.

The push for security autonomy is not going to happen by cutting off the U.S. defense contractors overnight. Realistically, defense budgets will expand, and the European defense contractors will rebuild the industry in parallel with European governments still buying equipment from the U.S. defense industry. I also believe that U.S. defense contractors will carefully assess opportunities to partner with European defense contractors to keep optimal access to the European market.

The opportunity in Europe is big. European leaders have agreed to free up €800 billion over a 4-year period for a temporary increase in the defense budgets. This will pave the way to kickstart an acceleration in defense spending and pave the way to spend more than 3% of GDP on defense by 2030.

The higher defense budgets in Europe will not solely benefit European defense contractors, but could also benefit U.S. defense contractors and could eventually even increase pressure on the U.S. to increase defense budgets more aggressively to remain a leading military power over the long term.

Commercial Aerospace Has A Long-Term Growth Trend

The current trade turmoil and diplomatic stress between the U.S. and its allies leads to a high degree of macroeconomic uncertainty and that may affect demand for commercial products. RTX derived 46% of its sales from commercial aerospace in 2024. So, the company does have some exposure that provides a risk during economic downturns. However, what we generally see is that big economic shocks such as the airline crisis in 2001, the financial crisis in 2008 and the pandemic in 2019 are overcome, and the commercial aerospace industry snaps back to its growth trends.

So, it is a very resilient market in which RTX has positive exposure through its Collins Aerospace business and aero engines. Currently, the Pratt & Whitney geared turbofan engines are providing pressure on RTX due to metal powder issues that have kept hundreds of airplanes grounded, but this should see significant improvement by 2027. Therefore, the company’s current performance is strong but still held back by the GTF issues.

In other words, RTX still has to fully unlock the earnings potential as it works its way through the engine issues and Boeing (unaffected by the GTF issues) and Airbus SE (OTCPK:EADSF) recover production rates. A risk for the business is that continued applications of tariffs may make the globally oriented aerospace supply chain less efficient.

Earnings Estimates Have Revised Downward, But RTX Has Strong Earnings Surprise History

RTX will report its first quarter results on the 22nd of April. Analysts are expecting the company to report revenues of $19.78 billion with core earnings per share of $1.36. Over the past two years, RTX has beaten analyst estimates on EPS every time while missing estimates on EPS only once.

Revenue estimates of $19.78 billion point at 2.5% growth and are revised downward by 1.8% over the past six months and the EPS estimates have come down by 2.2% and indicate 1.6% growth year-on-year.

RTX Stock Gets A Price Target Lift

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For RTX, I am lifting my price target from $145 to $149 and maintaining my buy rating. The increase in the stock price target is supported by the following items:

  • 2.8% higher EBITDA expected for 2025.
  • 1.5% higher EBITDA expected for 2026.
  • A marginal decrease of around 1%-2% in free cash flow for 2025 and 2026.
  • The stock remains undervalued compared to the peer group.
  • Additional $3 billion in available liquidity to return to shareholders by 2027. This has not yet been factored into the stock price calculation.

Overall, I am lifting my price target modestly and also have not implemented a price target for 2027 which is $164.55, and I do believe that in a more predictable market environment, RTX could see its trading multiple increase closer to the peer group. This would unlock additional upside.

The expected dividend yield of 1.9% is not necessarily compelling, but the company has a dividend growth streak of 31 years with an annual growth rate of 6.3% in the past five years. For long-term investors that provides some dividend income that over time could provide a nice yield on cost which I consider to be a superior measure to dividend yields.

Conclusion: RTX Corporation Remains Attractive

There is definitely some turmoil that could affect the defense and commercial end markets. However, I do believe that both end markets are actually growing, and they will more than likely grow over the longer term. So, defense will continue to provide stable growth while commercial aerospace provides somewhat higher growth opportunities through higher airplane production driving sales to original equipment manufacturers with a long-term tail for aftermarket revenues.

So, I remain bullish on the stock noting the end market growth trends and the resilience of RTX stock price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
